item.description: The suspected leader of one of Ecuador’s most feared gangs was shot dead in an ambush by gunmen holding teddy bears and flowers.

item.description: Ukraine's large-scale strike on Moscow, triggering massive explosions and fires, may indicate a new phase in the long-range air war.

item.title: UN reports record violations of children in conflict, with government forces the main perpetrators
item.description: Nearly 25,000 children caught in conflict were victims of a record number of violations last year, including killings, rape and recruitment to fight, and for the first time, government forces — not armed groups — were the main perpetrators, a new United Nations report says. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres’ annual report, released this week, has a blacklist of violators against children: government forces from eight nations and 67 armed groups from 16 countries and territories. The number of violations — which also include abductions, attacks on schools and hospitals, and denial of humanitarian access to help them — rose for a fourth straight year to 38,558, according to the report that is based on verified U.N. data.
item.dc:creator: AP

item.title: Shock and Awe in Moscow
item.description: A new wave of Ukrainian drone strikes in the heart of Russia’s capital city has exposed the weakness of Putin’s air defenses—and the potential fragility of his regime.
item.dc:creator: Puck News

item.title: On the Line: Guatemala Garment Workers Win Historic Payout
item.description: Also in this edition: South African workers protest the mass firing of their migrant colleagues, a Cambodian plant goes on strike, and contaminated water sickens hundreds of Bangladeshi workers.

item.title: Gaza is still a nightmare
item.description: What does it mean for a “ceasefire” to allow 1,000 people to be killed in Gaza?

item.title: Congo says number of confirmed Ebola cases rises to 896
item.description: June 18 () - The number of confirmed Ebola ‌cases in the Democratic ‌Republic of Congo has increased to ​896, including 232 deaths, government data showed late on Thursday. The number represented ‌the total ⁠number of confirmed cases as of Wednesday, ⁠according to a situation report that documented 21 ​new cases ​and six ​new deaths ‌in the previous 24 hours.

item.title: ICC sets July 24 vote on whether to remove chief prosecutor Karim Khan, WSJ reports
item.description: June 18 (Reuters) - The International Criminal Court has scheduled a vote for July 24 on whether to remove chief prosecutor Karim Khan from office, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday, citing

item.description: MEXICO CITY, June 18 (Reuters) - The Commission for Environmental Cooperation (CEC), which oversees environmental issues in the United States, Mexico and Canada, on Thursday said it will review a

item.title: Greece's Parthenon gets a facelift, revealing a look not seen for 220 years
item.description: Visitors to the Acropolis can now see the Parthenon’s western side looking whole for the first time in about 220 years. Officially unveiled Thursday, restorers have slotted two new marble blocks into long-empty gaps high on the temple’s western end — the view visitors see when they first enter the ancient monument in Athens. “They allow the unique proportions and the geometric perfection of the Parthenon’s western face to be seen once again,” she said.

item.title: Man arrested in UK after toddler ends up in zoo crocodile enclosure
item.description: Police in the UK have arrested a man on suspicion of attempted murder after a 3-year-old boy was found in a critical condition inside a zoo’s crocodile enclosure.

item.title: Jerusalem light rail service partially suspended due to haredi protests
item.description: An additional demonstration is being held near Prison 10 in protest of the arrest of a draft dodger earlier this week. Light rail service has been partially suspended between Jerusalem’s central bus station and Mount Herzl station on Thursday evening as haredi (ultra-Orthodox) protesters began protesting the arrest of a draft dodger earlier this week.

item.title: How Ukraine found a way through Moscow’s air defences
item.description: Hundreds of Ukrainian drones met little resistance as they soared through the Moscow skyline overnight and devastated a major Russian oil refinery.

item.title: Brown Bear Attacks Mountain Biker On Popular Alaskan Trail
item.description: A brown bear took a swipe at a mountain biker near Anchorage, last weekend.

item.title: EastEnders actress arrested after ‘trying to smuggle meth’ into Australia
item.description: A British actress who appeared in EastEnders has been arrested after allegedly trying to smuggle meth into Australia.

item.title: Mexican search group finds 25 unmarked graves while seeking Nancy Guthrie
item.description: The volunteer search organization spent days combing through a remote area roughly 70 miles from her Tucson home.

item.title: Cuba proposes sweeping reforms to socialist model amid US pressure
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Macron and Modi tour Indian pavilion at France's VivaTech
item.description: France highlighted its technology partnership with India on 18 June as President Emmanuel Macron and Prime Minister Narendra Modi toured VivaTech 2026 in Paris.

item.title: Dozens of countries warn of atrocities amid escalation in Sudan’s el-Obeid
item.description: UN and allies warn of grave rights violations in North Kordofan, ⁠urge maximum pressure on ⁠RSF and SAF.

item.title: Netanyahu rules out Israeli troop withdrawal from southern Lebanon
item.description: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on Thursday ruled out a withdrawal of Israeli troops from southern Lebanon for the foreseeable future. In a speech, Netanyahu described the "security zone" established by the Israeli military as a barrier between the Hezbollah militia in Lebanon and civilians and communities in northern Israel.

item.title: Massive blasts in Moscow shatter even Putin’s protective shell
item.description: News of the damage must surely have found its way into the most isolated of bunkers.

item.title: Iran's Khamenei approves deal, says Trump acted out of 'desperation'
item.description: Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei said in a letter shared by state media on Thursday that he approved the framework agreement with the United States but with reservations. Khamenei said he disagreed in "principle" with the deal but ultimately gave his approval out of a sense of responsibility toward the Iranian people and its allies.

item.title: CDC to tap $107m in emergency funding for Ebola response in DRC and Uganda
item.description: Number of people infected now tops 1,000 though health officials say the global risk remains low

item.title: Polish police arrest man for artist Semyon Skrepetsky's murder
item.description: Police in Poland arrested a suspect in the murder of Russian artist Robert Kuzovkov, 44, publicly known as Semyon Skrepetsky, who was killed Tuesday.

item.title: The First F-16 Block 70 Built For China's Air Force Was Seen Flying The Skies Of Texas
item.description: Sometimes, the military planes you see in the air aren't ours, but test flights of craft intended for other countries, like this F-16 spotted in Texas.

item.title: Cop Accused of Using AI to Fake Evidence
item.description: The officer allegedly used AI to "create evidential material in a number of cases."

item.title: Parents of Serbia's teenage school shooter given jail terms in retrial
item.description: The boy was 13 when he killed eight girls, a boy and a school guard at Vladislav Ribnikar school in Belgrade in 2023.

item.title: Peru’s president announces that Pope Leo will visit in early November
item.description: Peruvian President José María Balcázar announced Thursday that Pope Leo XIV will visit Peru in the first half of November. Balcázar said after meeting with the pontiff at the Vatican that Leo will travel to Puno, Iquitos, Cusco, Pucallpa, Piura and Chiclayo, where the Pope carried out his pastoral work for nearly a decade. The Chicago-born pope previously lived in Trujillo, on the northwestern coast, and became a Peruvian citizen in 2015. The Vatican has not confirmed any upcoming trip, but a visit to Peru with possible stops in other South American countries is widely rumored.

item.title: Russia Wants AI Sovereignty. It Has a Chip Problem
item.description: As Vladimir Putin's daughter helps lead a new push for AI talent, Russia's biggest obstacle may be the chips it can't make.

item.title: Ghana pushes for concrete slavery reparations
item.description: Ghana hosted a landmark global conference on Thursday seeking to translate growing political support for slavery reparations into practical commitments towards justice."The growing international support for these conversations demonstrates that reparatory justice is no longer a peripheral issue," Ghana's foreign minister said.

item.title: Israeli foreign minister calls out Kaja Kallas over apartheid remarks
item.description: Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar announced Thursday that he is cutting all contact with Kaja Kallas, EU foreign policy chief, after comments.

item.title: World's largest and smallest penny farthings unveiled in London
item.description: An Australian man teamed up with cyclists in Britain to break the Guinness World Records for the largest and smallest penny farthings.

item.title: Europe risks 'total irrelevance' without sovereign tech: Cohere chief
item.description: Europe risks "total technological irrelevance" if it fails to develop sovereign capabilities, the head of Canadian AI startup Cohere warned Thursday on the sidelines of the Vivatech trade fair in Paris.Since then it has also snapped up Canadian-German biomedical startup Reliant AI. And it signed partnerships in May with Spanish firms Indra, a defence specialist, and Multiverse computing, which develops AI software.

item.title: England’s Most Famous Tree, Linked to Robin Hood Legend, Has Died After 1,200 Years
item.description: Empires rose and fell. Kings came and went. For more than 1,200 years, it remained a living landmark that drew generations of pilgrims to stand in its ancient shadow. The Major Oak, a centuries-old tree long believed to have sheltered the legendary outlaw Robin Hood, has died, ending a remarkable run that spanned more than […]

item.title: Navy Finally Seeking To Dispose Of USS Long Beach, The World’s First Nuclear-Powered Cruiser
item.description: Tearing apart a nuclear-powered warship is a whole lot more costly and time-consuming than a conventionally powered one.

item.title: The World's Top Consumers Cause Up to $5.7 Trillion in Environmental Damage Every Year
item.description: The top 10% of global consumers have an outsized impact on the planet.

item.title: Russian warship risks refuelling disaster in Channel
item.description: The Russian warship that fired shots at a British yacht carried out multiple “dangerous” refuelling missions in the English Channel after almost running out of fuel.

item.title: Cuba leader admits 'urgent changes' needed to overcome crisis
item.description: Cuba's communist model needs "urgent changes" to overcome a major crisis which cannot be solely blamed on a crippling US oil blockade, President Miguel Diaz-Canel said in his frankest admission yet of the need for radical reforms.While Havana's impulse has been to blame its woes on a more-than-six-decade US trade embargo and more recent blockade, Diaz-Canel admitted in remarks broadcast Thursday there were "obstacles that don't come from outside, nor the blockade."

item.title: Accused in South African trial admits attack on missing German tourist
item.description: One of the four accused in a case concerning the disappearance of a German holidaymaker in Cape Town has confessed to robbing the tourist of his possessions, a South African court heard on Thursday. In a video clip played to the court in the Cape Town suburb of Wynberg, the man admits to being part of a group that attacked Nick Frischke and his friends on a mountain path in the area.

item.title: Ukraine: Strikes on Moscow are a good sign
item.description: Strikes on Moscow show Ukraine is “moving in the right direction”, the country’s defence minister has said.

item.title: 'Pure hell' in Moscow as Ukrainian drones strike major refinery supplying capital's fuel market
item.description: Ukraine launched one of its largest drone attacks on Moscow, striking a major oil refinery and sending thick black smoke over parts of the Russian capital.

item.title: Escaped German murderer crashes getaway motorcycle in Italy
item.description: A convicted murderer who escaped while out of prison on accompanied release has been found in an Italian hospital after crashing his getaway motorcycle, German police reported on Thursday. The 42-year-old man escaped on the motorcycle in Peine in the German state of Lower Saxony on Tuesday afternoon.

item.title: Weekly quiz: How many SpaceX employees just became millionaires?
item.description: How much attention did you pay to what happened in the world over the past seven days?

item.title: Stunning Video Shows Moscow Oil Refinery Roof Blasted into the Air After Ukrainian Drone Strikes
item.description: Stunning photos and video from Moscow showed multiple oil refineries on fire from Ukrainian drone strikes — including one that literally had the roof blown off.

item.title: Some EU leaders caution against talks with Russia
item.description: BRUSSELS, June 18 () - Several EU leaders said on Thursday there was no point in rushing into talks with Russia after an EU official said on Wednesday the office ‌of European Council President Antonio Costa had made "brief contacts at diplomatic level" with the Kremlin. European leaders ‌have recently stepped up discussions about the possibility of direct talks with Russia over the war in Ukraine after isolating Moscow ​diplomatically since it attacked Ukraine in February 2022.

item.title: Brazilian police probe senator close to Lula
item.description: Brazilian police investigating a banking scandal carried out raids Thursday against targets including a senator allied with President Luiz Inacio Lula de Silva, bringing the affair closer to his government as he seeks reelection in October.A police source said that the targets of these raids included Jaques Wagner, a close ally of Lula and his government's top representative in the Senate with the job of pushing the president's legislative agenda in that chamber.

item.title: Iran Orders Singer, Musicians Flogged Over YouTube Concert
item.description: Iran Orders Singer, Musicians Flogged Over YouTube Concert

item.title: UK removes ‘do not travel’ warnings for Dubai and several Middle Eastern countries
item.description: Holidays to Dubai and Abu Dhabi could be back on for British travellers this year

item.title: Israel kills at least three Palestinians in Gaza City drone strike
item.description: Gaza’s Health Ministry says at least 1,007 Palestinians have been killed by Israel since the so-called ‘ceasefire’.

item.title: South Africa builds another site to ease overcrowding and speed up deportation of Malawian nationals
item.description: South Africa started building a second temporary deportation center on Thursday to process the repatriation of Malawians after thousands protested earlier this year in Johannesburg and other parts of the country against illegal immigration that has fueled tensions between locals and foreign nationals. Meanwhile, thousands more Malawian nationals arrived at the first deportation center, also in the city of Durban, in an area known as Sherwood, where an estimated 10,000 have been camping for more than a week, waiting to be sent home. According to South African officials, at least 12 women had given birth at the site since Malawians started gathering there.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV to visit Peru in November
item.description: Peruvian President José María Balcázar confirmed Wednesday that Pope Leo XIV will visit Peru during the first half of November in a trip expected to last between eight and 10 days.

item.title: 2 sentenced for spying for Chinese government in Britain
item.description: British counter-terrorism police announced a Border Force officer and another have been sentenced for allegedly spying for the Chinese government.

item.title: Zimbabwean parliament extends President Mnangagwa's term
item.description: The Zimbabwean parliament on Thursday passed a law amending the constitution to extend the presidential term by two years and providing for the president to be elected by parliament rather than by popular vote. The measure, which was backed by 216 of 258 members in the National Assembly, still has to be approved by the country's Senate before being signed into law by President Emmerson Mnangagwa.

item.title: NK leaders' sister slams G7 call for denuclearization, defends nuclear arms as 'means of self-defense'
item.description: North Korean leader Kim Jong-un's sister denounced international calls for denuclearization on Thursday.

item.title: Ukraine hits Moscow refinery in major drone attack on Russian capital
item.description: Ukrainian drones hit the Russian capital’s oil refinery for the second time this week.

item.title: AI-generated videos wield Down syndrome to make sales
item.description: Videos appearing to show people with Down syndrome and urging users to buy from their struggling businesses pull in millions of views, but many of them are AI-generated fakes.Down Syndrome International complained to Meta about sexualized deep-fakes of people with Down syndrome and this resulted in the removal of many videos.

item.title: Watch: Beavers solve flooding problem at London Underground station
item.description: A London Underground station once plagued by constant flooding is now staying dry thanks to some recent arrivals in the neighborhood: beavers.

item.title: Suspected jihadists stage deadly new attack on Niger airport
item.description: An attack on the international airport in Niger's capital Thursday killed at least 11 soldiers and two civilians, officials said, months after jihadists staged a large assault on the sensitive site.Twenty assailants were killed and four soldiers wounded in the January 29 assault, authorities said.

item.title: ‘Navigating WW3’ to be discussed at secret meeting of world’s elites
item.description: A leaked agenda includes items such as “Money (Does?) Buy Happiness,” “Bring Back Nuclear,” and “How’s Your Sex Life?"

item.title: 'Electrical Non-Compliances' Found at Water Park After 'Tragic' Incident Left 12 Children Seriously Injured
item.description: Cultus Lake Waterpark said it will remain closed following the “electrical incident” that occurred near one of its waterslides earlier this week

item.title: "Lady Gucci," who had her ex-husband killed, could soon inherit $23M fortune
item.description: Despite spending 18 years in prison for orchestrating the murder of her ex-husband, fashion heir Maurizio Gucci, Patrizia Reggiani may be about to inherit a fortune.

item.title: Ukraine is shredding Russian supply lines. The US should pay attention
item.description: Ukraine’s drone campaign against Russian logistics is a preview of what U.S. forces could encounter in future conflicts.

item.title: Western countries warn of atrocities, imminent RSF escalation in Sudan's al-Obeid
item.description: By Olivia Le Poidevin GENEVA, June 18 (Reuters) - Dozens of countries including Britain, France and Germany raised the alarm at the U.N.

item.title: Admiral notes 'increasingly aggressive' Russian presence in the Baltic
item.description: The presence of Russian warships and security in the Baltic Sea region remains a particular focus for the German Navy, fleet commander Vice Admiral Axel Deertz, said on the sidelines of a conference in Kiel in northern Germany on Thursday. “This is an area where the security situation has noticeably intensified, where a potential adversary is quite active and is behaving in an increasingly aggressive manner,” the commander warned at the International Seapower Symposium in Kiel.

item.title: Zimbabwe vote to extend president's term underscores the staying power of Africa's aging leaders
item.description: Zimbabwe lawmakers voted on Thursday for constitutional changes that would defer elections and extend the tenure of the country’s 83-year-old president. The vote underscores the staying power of Africa's aging leaders on a continent that has some of the world’s oldest rulers despite boasting the youngest population globally. Zimbabwe's National Assembly overwhelmingly approved constitutional amendments that would postpone elections due in 2028 to 2030 and extend President Emmerson Mnangagwa’s term by two years.

item.title: Niger says soldiers, civilians killed in armed attack on Niamey airport
item.description: Ministry of Defence says 11 soldiers, 2 civilians and 22 attackers killed in Diori Hamani International Airport assault.

item.title: Lebanon: Tyre residents return to homes shattered by strikes
item.description: Lebanese families returning to Tyre found their homes in ruins on 18 June after Israeli strikes and voiced cautious optimism about a new US-Iran agreement.

item.title: Iran war leaves Islamic republic intact and opponents divided
item.description: The Iran war was cast as a catalyst for the Islamic republic's collapse, but months of fighting failed to dislodge the clerical leadership and left its opponents out in the cold.Rights groups and the United Nations have sounded the alarm over a surge in Iran in executions -- more than 40 since the war started -- and arrests in recent months, including many in connection with the protests the authorities called "terrorist riots".

item.title: French fishermen gather in Cherbourg as sector faces down multiple challenges
item.description: In total, the French government said €13 million would be dedicated to the fishing sector for fuel consumption in April and May.

item.title: Farmers deny charges over cow trampling death
item.description: Rebekah Morris, 29, was trampled to death by a cow while walking her dog Zero in 2022.

item.title: Maritime data company says stranded ships have begun transiting the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Major shipowners have begun moving vessels through the Strait of Hormuz since the U.S. and Iran signed an interim agreement to end their war, maritime data company Lloyd’s List Intelligence said Thursday. In a media briefing, Richard Meade, editor in chief of Lloyd’s List, said for the first time in 110 days, ships owned by major companies were crossing the strait after effectively being marooned there since February. Before the war, the waterway off Iran's coast carried a fifth of the world’s crude oil.

item.title: Foreign aid cuts push up migrant flows, IOM chief warns
item.description: Cuts in development aid by wealthy countries tend to drive up displacement away from the world's poorest regions, the head of the UN's International Organization for Migration warned in an interview with AFP on Thursday.Several rich Western countries -- particularly the United States but also many European nations -- have cut their development aid budgets in recent years, while also tightening migration policies and strengthening border controls.

item.title: Admiral notes 'increasingly aggressive' Russian presence in the Baltic
item.description: The presence of Russian warships and security in the Baltic Sea region remains a particular focus for the German Navy, fleet commander Vice-Admiral Axel Deertz, said on the sidelines of a conference in Kiel in northern Germany on Thursday. “This is an area where the security situation has noticeably intensified, where a potential adversary is quite active and is behaving in an increasingly aggressive manner,” the commander warned at the International Seapower Symposium in Kiel.

item.title: 'Do not travel' advice for Dubai dropped
item.description: Thousands of Brits were left stranded in the Middle East when the US-Iran war broke out in early 2026.

item.title: Ebola cases increase almost 40% in a week as death toll passes 200
item.description: The Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda has claimed more than 200 lives in its first month and is the worst known outbreak at this stage, with up to 35,000 suspected potential contacts, Africa’s Centres for Disease Control and Prevention said on Thursday. With 894 confirmed cases so far, the current outbreak is three times worse than a previous outbreak in Uganda in 2000, which had 281 cases at the same point, said Dr. Wessam Mankoula, a medical epidemiologist at Africa CDC. The number of cases has increased 38% since last week and is now in 32 health zones across eastern Congo, said Mankoula.

item.title: Inquest Into Influencer Stacey Warnecke’s ‘Freebirth’ Death Comes to a Halt as Critical New Evidence Emerges: Report
item.description: The content creator died on Sept. 29, hours after giving birth to her first son

item.title: Girl, 5, and Her Brother, 1, Fatally Struck by SUV While Walking Home from School with Their Mom
item.description: "I wish it hit me, not my babies," the children's mother said after the deadly crash

item.title: Polish police arrest suspect in killing of exiled Russian artist
item.description: Polish police have arrested a suspect in the fatal shooting of an exiled Russian artist and Kremlin critic, four days after the attack, authorities said on Thursday. Investigators are still examining whether anyone assisted the suspect in planning the killing or helping him flee, a spokesman for police in the eastern Lublin region said.

item.title: Italy lacks evidence for now to seek trial in Sarajevo sniper probe, source says
item.description: MILAN, June 18 () - Italian prosecutors investigating accusations of "sniper tourism" in 1990s Sarajevo do not currently have enough evidence to seek a trial for any suspects, a source with direct ‌knowledge of the matter said on Thursday. Milan prosecutors have placed five people under investigation over ‌allegations that Italians and other foreigners paid to shoot at civilians during the 1992-95 siege of Sarajevo, the source said.

item.title: Ashes of Missing College Student Found Dead in Japan Will Be Scattered ‘Right Where He Wants to Be,’ Says Reverend
item.description: Two benches in Weston Higginbotham’s honor will also be installed at Oak Mountain State Park in Alabama

item.title: Keeping up appearances: Greece reveals Parthenon façade after 220 years
item.description: For the first time in about 220 years, the west façade of the Parthenon in Greece can be see in its fullest possible form. External scaffolding has now been permanently removed from the monument, marking a key step in its long restoration.View on euronews

item.title: US family returns four artifacts to Egypt, including statue head thought to depict female pharoah
item.description: The artifacts were received in Cairo on June 5 before being transferred to the Egyptian Museum for further examination, documentation and restoration ahead of their display in the museum. Four ancient artifacts were returned to Egypt by an American family in early June, announced the Egyptian Tourism and Antiquities Ministry.

item.title: Oil tankers pass Hormuz Strait after war deal: tracker
item.description: Three big Saudi oil tankers left the Gulf through the Hormuz Strait on Thursday, maritime trackers said, after leaders vowed to reopen the route under an agreement to end the US-Iran war.Among them were three very large crude carriers (VLCC), each transporting two million barrels of oil from Saudi Arabia, the tracker said.

item.title: Zimbabwe lawmakers back legislation extending president's time in power
item.description: By Chris Takudzwa Muronzi and Nelson Banya HARARE, June 18 (Reuters) - Zimbabwe's lower house of parliament passed a bill to extend presidential terms from five to seven years on Thursday, which would

item.title: Why fines for going topless are becoming more common in France’s holiday destinations
item.description: While being topless on beaches are still allowed, authorities are clamping down on those wandering around town centres without a shirt

item.title: Israeli attacks kill three people in southern Lebanon
item.description: At least three people have been killed in renewed Israeli attacks in southern Lebanon, Lebanese reports said on Thursday. Two people were killed in an Israeli drone strike on a town south-east of Nabatiyeh in the south, the state news agency NNA reported.

item.title: Ukraine and Russia again exchange bodies of fallen
item.description: Moscow and Kiev have again exchanged bodies of the fallen in their four-year war, the Ukrainian authorities reported on Thursday. Ukraine received 522 bodies.

item.title: Western façade of Parthenon restored after 200-year project
item.description: The restoration of the western façade of the Parthenon has been completed, marking the culmination of a project that has spanned more than two centuries, Greek Culture Minister Lina Mendoni said on Thursday. Scaffolding on the western side of the ancient temple on the Acropolis in Athens has now been removed, revealing the western façade for the first time in more than 200 years.

item.title: Russian nuclear chief accuses Ukraine of killing worker at Zaporizhzhia power plant in drone attack
item.description: MOSCOW, June 18 () - The head of Russia's state nuclear corporation Rosatom on Thursday ‌accused Ukraine of killing a key worker ‌in a drone attack on the Russian-controlled Zaporizhzhia nuclear power ​plant in southeastern Ukraine. There was no immediate response from Ukraine and could not independently verify the statement.

item.title: Novelist Kundera and wife buried in Czech home city
item.description: The remains of Czech-French novelist Milan Kundera and his wife Vera were buried in the writer's native city of Brno on Thursday, the city hall said."The burial was held in a narrow circle of family members, friends, French embassy staff and representatives of Brno, a city of which Kundera was an honorary citizen," the Brno city hall said on its website.

item.title: He Wanted to Study Business. He Ended Up Romance-Scamming Older Women. He Told Me Everything.
item.description: First, it was the Nigerian prince. Then the distressed soldier. It’s gotten even worse.

item.title: Vietnam War hero of Dong Ha Bridge to be awarded the Medal of Honor
item.description: President Donald Trump is set to posthumously award Marine Col. John Ripley the Medal of Honor on June 18 in a White House ceremony.

item.title: Germany fan takes legal action over viral AI Hitler image at World Cup
item.description: A Germany fan is taking legal action after a fake AI-generated photo of him resembling Adolf Hitler in the crowd at a World Cup game went viral on social media. "I just can't believe it," Jan Weitzel told German public broadcaster hr3 on Thursday, days after the German side's 7-1 victory over Curaçao in Houston.

item.title: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains subdued
item.description: Following the signing of the framework agreement between the United States and Iran, shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains subdued for the moment. A spokesman for the Association of German Shipowners (VDR) told dpa that the association could not currently detect any significant increase in traffic.

item.title: Strikes to hit over half of IKEA stores in Germany amid labour dispute
item.description: IKEA outlets in Germany are facing widespread strikes, the Verdi trade union said on Thursday. The walkouts on Friday are likely to affect over half of the Swedish furniture giant's stores in the country, Verdi said.

item.title: Germany fan takes legal action over viral AI Hitler image at World Cup
item.description: A Germany fan is taking legal action after a fake AI-generated photo of him resembling Adolf Hitler in the crowd at a World Cup game went viral on social media. "I just can't believe it," Jan Weitzel told German public broadcaster hr3 on Thursday, days after the German side's 7-1 victory over Curaçao in Houston.

item.title: Three charged after record-breaking £139m cannabis seizure
item.description: Three people from south Wales are charged after £139m of cannabis shipped from Canada to UK seized.

item.title: Body found in search for missing California woman Ashley Phillips who vanished in Costa Rica
item.description: A body has been found in the area where a California woman went missing in Costa Rica.

item.title: Acropolis façade visible again after a 200-year restoration job
item.description: The restoration of the western gable of the Parthenon on the Acropolis in Athens has been completed after around 200 years, with the outer scaffolding on the west side of the ancient temple also removed for good. Repairs to the Acropolis first began in the mid-19th century and the west facade is now fully visible for the first time since restoration work began two centuries ago, Greek Culture Minister Lina Mendoni announced on Thursday.

item.title: Cuban economy needs 'urgent changes' to overcome crisis: president
item.description: Cuba's economy needs "urgent changes" to overcome a major crisis intensified by a US oil blockade, President Miguel Diaz-Canel said in a speech to Communist Party leaders broadcast on Thursday.The oil blockade imposed by President Donald Trump in January has brought Cuba's already moribund economy to the brink of collapse, marked by power cuts sometimes lasting over 30 hours and shortages of food, fuel, drinking water and medicine.

item.title: US-Iran deal leaves the future of Lebanon uncertain – and subject to Israel playing the spoiler
item.description: President Donald Trump has talked of a potential role for Syrian forces in fighting Hezbollah – a move that would raise alarm in Lebanon.

item.title: China's Cai Qi cements spot as Xi confidant as head of Communist Party school
item.description: BEIJING, June 18 (Reuters) - China officially appointed its fifth-ranked leader Cai Qi as head of the Communist Party's central ideology school, cementing his position as one of President Xi Jinping's

item.title: UK to provide 150,000 drones to Ukraine as it steps up Russia strikes
item.description: New UK Defence Secretary Dan Jarvis has announced Britain’s biggest-ever package of drones for Ukraine. The UK will provide 150,000 drones and 350 air defence missiles and radars to the war-torn nation by the end of the year.

item.title: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains subdued
item.description: Following the signing of the framework agreement between the United States and Iran, shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains subdued for the moment. A spokesman for the Association of German Shipowners (VDR) told dpa that the association could not currently detect any significant increase in traffic.

item.title: The European Union has quietly sought to reopen communication with Russia
item.description: The European Union has reached out to Moscow in a tentative bid to open a line of communication so the continent is not sidelined in any potential talks to end Russia’s grinding war in Ukraine, officials said Thursday. The news emerged as Ukraine launched one of its biggest drone attacks since Russia’s all-out invasion of its neighbor more than four years ago, Russian officials said Thursday. A major Moscow oil refinery was hit for a second time in a week and commercial flights at Moscow airports were disrupted.

item.title: Ex-detective sentenced over prison drone drug drops
item.description: Prohibited items were flown into prisons in Northamptonshire and Leicestershire, police say.

item.description: The drop in funding has also led donors to prioritize the trend of using aid to control the movement of people – over their needs.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz traffic ramps up in wake of U.S.-Iran deal signing
item.description: More tankers are transiting the Strait of Hormuz after 111 days of gridlock, but Iran and the U.S. still have much to discuss.

item.title: A Farmer Kicked a Cougar to Save Her Goat, and the Whole Thing Was Caught on Camera
item.description: A farm owner in British Columbia engaged in a courageous fight with a cougar to save one of her baby goats. Her security camera caught the whole thing. item.title: Spanish king to visit Mexican president on June 25 as ties improve
item.description: Spain's King Felipe VI will visit Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum on June 25 during a World Cup trip in another sign of the countries' diplomatic thaw, the royal palace said on Thursday.Felipe "will make a stopover in Mexico City on Thursday, June 25 to accept the invitation of President Sheinbaum and hold a bilateral meeting in the National Palace", the palace said in a statement.

item.title: Putin’s adoring crowd accidentally outed as ‘extras’ by bodyguard
item.description: When Vladimir Putin emerged from the Cathedral of the Annunciation in Kazan, a city in south-west Russia, he was greeted by well-wishers keen to shake his hand and tell him what a good job he was doing.

item.description: Ukraine said last month that Russia had relocated a significant number of air defense systems to Moscow.

item.title: Residents return to war-ravaged southern Lebanon with hope and sorrow after the US-Iran deal
item.description: Adnan Kaour returned on Thursday to check on his home in southern Lebanon 's coastal city of Tyre — once known as an idyllic summer getaway spot — just a week after Israel issued warnings for all of its residents to evacuate. The warnings were followed by sweeping airstrikes on the city, which Israel said targeted the Lebanese Hezbollah militant group. What Kaour found back in Tyre, shattered his hopes — his dream family apartment overlooking the shimmering Mediterranean Sea was a heap of rubble and shattered glass.

item.title: Sunken wreck of WWII ‘hell ship’ used to transport POWs discovered after more than 80 years
item.description: Archaeologists have discovered the wreck of the Hōfuku Maru — the final resting place of 1,000 British and Dutch POWs captured in Singapore during World War II.

item.description: China was running a secret espionage operation a stone’s throw away from the British Museum.

item.title: German police investigate attack on far-right AfD politician
item.description: German police are investigating an alleged attack on a regional politician from the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD). Michael Meister, a member of the parliament in the north-eastern state of Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, is said to have been approached by two unknown men in the city of Rostock early on Thursday morning, before being attacked and injured on the arm.

item.title: Secret Hamas records given to 'Post' expose covert deception plan behind Oct. 7
item.description: The documents, provided by the Meir Amit Institute, detail the progression of Hamas's secret plans to systematically deceive Israel into complacency before the October 7 massacre. New secret Hamas internal documents have been provided by IDF intelligence to the Meir Amit Terrorism and Intelligence Research Institute, which provided them exclusively to The Jerusalem Post on Thursday.

item.description: Germany is among a number of countries that have offered help in securing the shipping lane, but only if an Iran ceasefire deal turns out to be lasting.

item.description: Ukraine's Zelenskyy says after second major strike on Russian oil refinery that unless Putin stops his war, "Moscow will burn."

item.description: Authorities in Catalonia confirmed that the 63-year-old died in the Palau de Noguera area on Wednesday.

item.description: The boy is the ninth person to be arrested in connection with the fatal stabbing of Jamal Ringrose.

item.title: Israel seized more land from neighbors since 2023 than it has in decades
item.description: Over the past two and a half years, Israel has taken control of swaths of Gaza, Lebanon and Syria that amount to its biggest expansion of militarily occupied lands in decades. It is an area larger than many major cities — roughly 1,000 square kilometers (386 square miles) — and Israel has said it plans to stay indefinitely. The land seizures began in the aftermath of Hamas’ 2023 cross-border attack, which ignited wars on multiple fronts.

item.title: S.African sentenced in 'world's largest' rhino trafficking case
item.description: A South African court has convicted and fined a rhino horn trafficking "mastermind", ending a trial spanning 15 years, police said Thursday, describing it as the world's largest such case.The case was "regarded as the world's largest rhino horn trafficking investigation" with Groenewald as "the mastermind behind the large-scale rhino horn trafficking enterprise," the statement said.

item.title: Suspect in killing of a Russian artist critical of Putin has been arrested in Poland
item.description: Polish authorities have arrested a man suspected of fatally shooting a Russian activist critical of President Vladimir Putin and Chechen leader Ramzan Kadyrov, and are investigating whether Russia is behind it, senior officials said Thursday. The killing is the latest act which Polish authorities believe could be part of a campaign of Russian sabotage aimed at sowing fear and demoralizing Ukraine's closest allies. Poland, a NATO and European Union member, has in recent years become a place of refuge for political dissidents from Russia and Belarus, as well as Ukrainian war refugees.

item.title: China says ‘spy turtles’ are snooping in its waters
item.description: China's Ministry of State Security released an article Friday on social media platform WeChat, noting an "invisible secret war" is ongoing.

item.title: Putin and leaders of Southeast Asia agree to bolster ties at a summit in Russia
item.description: President Vladimir Putin on Thursday hailed Russia's ties with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations as he hosted a summit intended to strengthen economic and political ties with the bloc. The leaders who took part in the meeting in Kazan agreed to further expand “strategic partnership” between Russia and ASEAN nations that include Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, East Timor and Vietnam. “It is a strategic partnership that serves as an essential stabilizing factor in the Asia-Pacific amidst geopolitical turbulence, contributing to the formation of a balanced security architecture and equitable mutually beneficial cooperation,” Putin said at the summit, which marked the 35th anniversary of Russia-ASEAN relations.

item.description: If Manchester mayor Andy Burnham wins the vote, he will seek to oust Prime Minister Keir Starmer.

item.title: Factbox-From Australia to Europe, countries move to curb children's social media access
item.description: GDANSK, June 18 () - Australia in December became the world's first country to ban social media for children under 16, blocking them from platforms including TikTok, Alphabet's YouTube and Meta's Instagram and Facebook. Below is a summary of what countries and companies are doing to regulate access to social media amid mounting concerns over the impact of social media on children's health and safety.

item.title: Church of England apologizes for role in forced adoptions as recent as the mid-1970s
item.description: The Church of England apologized Thursday for its role in forced adoptions as recent as the mid-1970s, acknowledging the painful experiences of many unmarried women at so-called mother and baby homes in the U.K. that were affiliated with the church. Archbishop of Canterbury Sarah Mullally, the first woman to lead the church and the person seen as the spiritual leader of the worldwide Anglican Communion, issued the apology as the church released a report on conditions at the homes from 1949 to 1976.

item.title: Lawyer in high-profile Ugandan treason case charged with related offence
item.description: Uganda's military chief had boasted of "the hurt and pain" he would inflict on Erias Lukwago, who was arrested earlier this week.

item.description: A refinery and a shopping centre were burning after almost 200 Ukrainian drones struck an area to the south-east of the Russian capital.

item.title: Gunmen attack airport in Niger's capital as explosions, gunfire heard
item.description: Gunmen attacked the main airport in Niger’s capital of Niamey early Thursday morning, leading to an exchange of fire and explosions, witnesses and a security official said. Security forces were deployed to repel the attack after the gunmen breached the airport security, said the official, who was not authorized to speak publicly about the situation. It was the second attack at Diori Hamani International Airport in Niamey this year, after the Islamic State group claimed responsibility for a similar attack in January that targeted Niger's drone assets.

item.title: Europe Today: NATO, Ukraine and the battle over Europe's future budget
item.description: NATO defence ministers are gathering to prepare for next month’s leaders’ summit in Ankara. Among those attending is Pentagon chief and US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.

item.description: A suspect has been arrested after the fatal shooting of an exiled Russian artist in Poland, Prime Minister Donald Tusk said on Thursday. The man, who holds a Georgian passport, may have been involved in the crime, Tusk said in Warsaw, according to the PAP news agency.

item.title: China welcomes US-Iranian framework agreement
item.description: China on Thursday welcomed the framework agreement between the United States and Iran on ending the current war. The signing of the memorandum of the initial phase was of "positive significance for easing tensions and consolidating the ceasefire momentum," a foreign ministry spokesman said in Beijing.

item.title: Slovakia’s government faces confidence vote as debt exceeds constitutional limit
item.description: The Slovak government is set to face a parliamentary confidence vote Thursday that was triggered by the country’s debt surpassing fiscal limits set by the Constitution. The motion was called after the Constitutional Court, the country’s highest authority, ruled Wednesday that the government has to ask for such a vote without delay. Populist Prime Minister Robert Fico said he respected the court's ruling and called for the vote.

(Reporting ​by Abu Sultan ​in ‌Bengaluru; editing ​by Barbara ​Lewis).

item.title: Germany 'ready' for Hormuz mission as minesweeper heads to Red Sea
item.description: A German minesweeper is passing through the Suez Canal on its way to the Red Sea ahead of a possible deployment in the Strait of Hormuz, Defence Minister Boris Pistorius said on Thursday. Speaking in Brussels ahead of a meeting with his NATO counterparts, Pistorius confirmed that two German Navy ships - the minesweeper Fulda and a supply vessel, the Mosel - are travelling towards the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Ukraine sets Moscow refinery ablaze in biggest attack in years

item.title: Year-old video of drill participants falsely linked to Pakistan-administered Kashmir clashes
item.description: Violent clashes between members of a banned protest movement and police in Pakistan-administered Kashmir in June 2026 left dozens dead and wounded, but posts claiming the Joint Awami Action Committee (JAAC) supporters were faking their injuries are misrepresenting old, unrelated footage. The video used in the posts was filmed a year earlier in Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province and shows people who had taken part in a mock drill organised by emergency services.

item.title: Clouds of black smoke rise over Moscow after Ukrainian drones hit an oil refinery
item.description: Ukraine hit a major Moscow oil refinery for a second time in a week and disrupted commercial flights at the city's airports in one of its biggest drone attacks since Russia’s all-out invasion of its neighbor more than four years ago, Russian officials said Thursday. The attack came hours after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said he had held “an important coordination call” with the presidents of the United States and France, and had won key pledges of further support from the G7 summit this week. The footage in fact shows a deadly fire at a housing complex in Hong Kong in November 2025.

item.title: AI-generated images falsely depict Rodrigo Duterte attending ICC hearing
item.description: Former Philippine president Rodrigo Duterte's new lead legal counsel has sought another assessment of his fitness to stand trial in his crimes against humanity case, but images shared online showing him appearing frail inside a courtroom are fabricated. Duterte has not physically attended a hearing at the International Criminal Court (ICC) and the images, which contain visual artefacts indicative of synthetic content, were flagged as made with Google's AI tools.

item.description: A string of west African countries have outlawed same-sex relations in recent months, further eroding LGBTQ rights on a continent where they were already under attack.He linked the laws to mounting anti-Western sentiment -- seen, for example, in France's strained ties with its former west African colonies, particularly the military juntas that have seized power in recent years in Burkina Faso, Mali and Niger.

item.title: The end to years of brutal fighting is in sight for some of Ukraine's battle-weary troops
item.description: Kyiv hopes to start discharging some of its longest-serving troops by late autumn, giving them a reprieve many expected to come only at the war's end.

item.title: Ukraine hopes renewables can Russia-proof power grid
item.description: Quick to build and able to power a small city, the Oriv wind farm in western Ukraine is exactly the kind of project Kyiv hopes will backstop its power grid against routine Russian strikes."It is an incredible support for the energy system in difficult times," Greenpeace coordinator Marine Abramyan told AFP from the Oriv wind farm, in Ukraine's western Lviv region.

item.title: The pressure to have baby boys can harm African mothers' health
item.description: When Prosper Mbumba married, he expected to have only two children. “In my tribe, in my culture, that was like an insult, having only daughters,” the human rights activist said. Mbumba, sitting with his wife in an open-air bar in the Congolese capital of Kinshasa, described finally feeling a “little satisfied.”

item.title: AI visuals of fighting Australian politicians lure viewers to investment scam
item.description: AI-generated visuals of a brawl between Australian politicians Angus Taylor and Jacqui Lambie on an ABC News show keep appearing for social media users in Australia, with the fabricated narrative designed to get users to sign up for a scam trading platform. The public broadcaster told AFP the posts seek "to exploit the trust audiences place in the ABC's brands", while the two politicians have denounced the use of their likeness for scams.

item.title: A jailed billionaire’s Birkin bags are going on sale. It won’t be enough to repay her victims

item.title: Drastic restrictions on public transport take effect in Cuba
item.description: Drastic restrictions on cross-country travel took effect Thursday in cash-strapped Cuba, with spaces on ever scarcer trains and buses now reserved for the sick, people traveling for funerals and other emergencies.From Thursday, trains from the capital Havana to cities in the east will only run every 16 days - compared to around three times a week previously.

item.title: North Korea recalls its ambassador to Britain after sanctions on children's camp, report says
item.description: SEOUL, June 18 () - North Korea has recalled its ambassador to Britain just a month after he took ‌up the post, downgrading diplomatic relations in response ‌to British sanctions on a children's camp, North Korea-focused website NK News reported. item.title: Equatorial Guinea government resigns after missing targets, vice president says
item.description: The entire Cabinet in Equatorial Guinea has resigned after failing to achieve the vast majority of the government's goals, Vice President Teodoro Nguema Obiang Mangue said. Obiang, who is also the son of President Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo, said Tuesday that Prime Minister Manuel Osa Nsue Nsuga had submitted the Cabinet's resignation after falling far short of government targets. The ruling Democratic Party of Equatorial Guinea, or PDGE, said the president was dissatisfied with the government’s performance, citing corruption, delays in development projects and a failure to diversify the economy.

item.title: Former Egyptian doctor detained over hospital abuse allegations, released on bail

Authorities raided the home of Omnia Swaydan in Damanhour, Beheira province, around 6 p.m. local time on Tuesday while she was alone and ordered her to turn off her phone before taking her into custody, according to Asmaa Naeim, a lawyer following her case. Swaydan appeared Wednesday afternoon at the prosecutor’s office in Alexandria for questioning, Naeim told The Associated Press.

item.title: UN food agencies warn acute hunger will worsen in 13 hot spots as famine risks rise
item.description: The United Nations’ food agencies warned Wednesday that acute hunger is set to worsen across 13 global hot spots in the coming months, with conflict, funding shortages and climate shocks pushing millions closer to famine. The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the World Food Program (WFP) said in a new joint report conditions are expected to deteriorate between June and November 2026, with around 266 million people already facing high levels of acute food insecurity, and called for urgent action.

item.description: Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni on Wednesday urged Israel to be a "positive player" for peace in the Middle East following a deal between Iran and the US. "Our goal must be to promote lasting, structural solutions that go beyond the logic of short-term truces," Meloni said as the G7 summit in France came to a close.Asked about heavy criticism from Trump of her position during the Middle East war, Meloni said that she and the US president were both "quite strong characters".

item.title: China detains US citizen and Myanmar activist Min Zin
item.description: For decades, Min Zin stood among Myanmar's best-known advocates for democratic reform, surviving exile after the military crushed the country's 1988 pro-democracy uprising and later building a career as a respected scholar and policy analyst. Now the U.S. citizen, University of California, Berkeley doctoral candidate and founder of a leading Myanmar think tank is being held in China on suspicion of espionage after traveling to Yunnan province earlier this month. Chinese authorities have disclosed few details about the allegations, prompting concern from U.S. officials, human rights groups and academic observers.

item.title: Uganda court charges lawyer for jailed Ugandan opposition leader with concealing treason
item.description: A court in Uganda on Wednesday charged an attorney for a jailed opposition leader with concealing treason, escalating a dispute with the country's army chief whom the lawyer sought to hold accountable for alleged abuses. Erias Lukwago was charged before a magistrate's court in Kampala, the Ugandan capital, days after he was seized and taken into custody on the orders of army chief Gen. Muhoozi Kainerugaba. Lukwago was charged with “misprision of treason,” a crime that the presiding magistrate said stems from his alleged failure to report acts of treason by others.

item.title: More than 1,000 people have been killed in Gaza during ceasefire, Health Ministry says
item.description: DEIR AL-BALAH, Gaza Strip (AP) — Israeli operations in the Gaza Strip have killed 1,005 Palestinians since a ceasefire was reached between Israel and the militant group Hamas last October, the Gaza Health Ministry said Wednesday. The enclave has seen near-daily strikes, as well as shelling and gunfire along the boundary that divides Gaza into Israeli and Palestinian-controlled zones. The most recent deaths were recorded after a series of Israeli drone strikes in the past few days on towns and refugee camps in central Gaza and Gaza City.

item.title: Belgian driver gets 27-year jail term for deadly carnival crash

item.title: 15 countries in Kenya adopt the Mombasa Declaration to fight illegal fishing
item.description: Fifteen countries from Africa, Asia, Europe, the Caribbean, and the Pacific adopted a declaration on Wednesday to step up efforts to combat illegal fishing, a practice that experts say costs the world economy up to $50 billion annually. The Mombasa Declaration, named after the Kenyan city hosting the 11th Our Ocean Conference, calls on governments to improve access to information on fishing vessels, ownership and licensing, and to strengthen data sharing to better track fishing activities and enforce regulations. Out of the more than 30 countries represented in the summit, Belgium, Cameroon, Chile, the Dominican Republic, France, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, Liberia, Panama, Papua New Guinea, Peru, the Republic of the Congo, Somalia and South Korea signed the agreement.

item.title: Microhistory pioneer Carlo Ginzburg, who gave voice to the marginalized, dies at 87
item.description: Carlo Ginzburg, an Italian historian whose pioneering work transformed the study of the past by recovering the voices of marginalized people, died Wednesday at 87. The Scuola Normale Superiore in Pisa, where he was both a student and professor emeritus, said he died in the northern Italian city of Bologna. Ginzburg was a pioneer of microhistory, which focuses on small, specific units of analysis — such as an individual, a community, or a singular event — to reveal broader themes and issues within history.

item.title: A 16-month-old and his mother recover from Ebola in rare good news from outbreak in Congo
item.description: A 16-month-old baby and his mother have recovered from Ebola in eastern Congo, a rare positive development as Africa's top health body warned the outbreak of the deadly virus could become the worst on record if it continues to spread. The two left the Rwampara Treatment Center on Tuesday, near Bunia, in Ituri province, the epicenter of the outbreak, along with five other people who also recovered from Ebola. “The joy is immense given the state he was in at first,” Kahindo Mireille Pierrette of her infant.
